summary: Gruber analyzes Amazon's new Kindle lineup, arguing that the Kindle Fire succeeds where other tablet makers failed by offering a clear value proposition: cheap hardware backed by Amazon's vast content ecosystem. Rather than competing directly with the iPad on specs and price, Amazon built an alternative that attacks from the low end, using its retail strength the way Apple uses its hardware design strength. Gruber also reflects on the enduring value of e-ink Kindles, praising the new Kindle Touch for fixing everything wrong with previous models. He notes Amazon likely sells Kindle hardware at cost or a loss, confident content sales will recoup the investment — the razor-and-blades model that competitors like Samsung and Motorola simply cannot replicate.
key_points: The Kindle Fire is the first non-iPad tablet with a compelling answer to 'why buy this instead of an iPad?' — it's cheaper and backed by Amazon's content ecosystem
Apple and Amazon approach the tablet market from opposing ends: Apple from premium hardware, Amazon from low-cost retail devices optimized for purchasing content
Competitors like Samsung, Motorola, and RIM fail because they can't match Apple's hardware or Amazon's content — they have neither razors nor blades
E-ink Kindles retain tremendous value even in a post-iPad world because they offer a different mental mode — peaceful reading disconnected from the always-on computing experience
Amazon's 'special offers' ad-supported Kindles are now the default, with ad-free models commanding a premium — a shrewd inversion of last year's framing
money_quotes: It's all about the content, though. That's the difference that other tablet makers missed.
Amazon built an alternative to the iPad, rather than a direct competitor. It's a different market segment.
The Kindles are to Amazon what the printed catalog was to Sears a century ago.
Amazon can give away the razor because they're already in the business of selling blades. The other guys don't even have blades to sell.
E-ink feels peaceful to me. The Kindle doesn't feel like a computer.
It feels — not to the touch but to the eyes and mind — like a crudely-typeset and slightly smudgily-printed paper book. That's a good thing.
My seven-year-old son refuses to believe me that my Kindle is not broken, because its screen doesn't respond to touches. He's not far off base.
Attack from a position of strength. Build on your previous successes. That's what Apple does. That's what Amazon is doing here.
key_insight: Amazon succeeded where every other iPad competitor failed by not competing with the iPad at all — instead building a low-cost content-consumption device that plays to Amazon's retail strengths rather than chasing Apple's hardware strengths.
